summary: Camille Fournier writes to her team explaining the purpose and value of performance reviews despite their reputation as a flawed process. She emphasizes that reviews serve multiple purposes: celebrating accomplishments, developing self-promotion skills, receiving honest feedback on areas for improvement, reinforcing company values, and setting goals for growth. She encourages team members to take the process seriously both as recipients and as reviewers, noting that some of her most valuable career feedback came through this process. The letter frames reviews as an investment in personal development rather than a bureaucratic exercise.
key_points: Celebrating successes is as valuable as identifying areas for improvement—learning happens when we study both
Self-promotion is a career skill worth developing because not all future managers will track your accomplishments
Written reviews allow reflection on year-long trends and patterns that real-time feedback often misses
Performance reviews reinforce shared company values and remind everyone what the organization stands for
Goal setting during reviews creates structured opportunities to discuss career direction with your manager
money_quotes: There is as much value in knowing what you are good at and what others appreciate in you as there is in knowing what to change.
Self-promotion feels bad to some people, but it is important to learn the skill enough to write about your accomplishments.
You will have many managers throughout your career and they will not always have this ability.
This isn't the time to be petty but it is the time to be thoughtful and honest.
Ask me about the time I got review feedback that told me I was creating a 'culture of fear.'
Learning happens when we study our successes as well as our failures.
The prose is less important than the content, a bulleted list that says something meaningful is fine if that is your style.
key_insight: Performance reviews, despite their flaws, provide unique value by forcing structured reflection on accomplishments, trends in behavior, and career direction that regular feedback cycles rarely capture.
